title: Kali, Durga, and the Spiritual Permission of Rage
dek: *The Hindu goddesses of destruction teach what Western psychology is only beginning to understand: that anger held in the body becomes disease, and anger honored becomes discernment. This is about reclaiming the intelligence of your fire without apology, and the somatic practices that make that possible.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Kali and Durga carry a sacred teaching about rage as protection, boundary-setting, and fierce love rather than pathology. Suppressed anger becomes inflammation, chronic tension, and disconnection from your own truth and power. Learning to move with your anger instead of against it restores vitality, voice, and the capacity to defend what matters.

What would happen if you let yourself feel the full heat of your rage without collapsing into shame about it? Most of us were taught early that anger makes us bad. Unlovable. Out of control. So we learned to smile when we wanted to scream, to accommodate when we wanted to push back, to hold our breath and tighten our jaws and swallow the words that needed saying. We became very good at not feeling what we felt. And then we wondered why our necks hurt, why our digestion failed, why we felt so tired all the time. Kali and Durga stand at the threshold of a different possibility. These goddesses don't apologize for their fury. They wield it. Kali, draped in severed heads and dancing on the corpse of ego, is the force that destroys what needs to die. Durga, riding her lion into battle, is the mother who will obliterate anything that threatens her children. Both are depicted with weapons raised, eyes blazing, tongues out in challenge. And both are worshipped not in spite of their rage but because of it. This isn't metaphor. It's permission. ## The Goddesses Who Refuse to Be Nice In Hindu cosmology, Kali emerges when the gods cannot defeat the demon Raktabija, whose every drop of spilled blood births a new warrior. The male gods are helpless. So Durga, already fierce, gives birth to Kali, who drinks the blood before it hits the ground and devours the demons whole. She is the answer to an impossible problem. She is what happens when niceness has failed and something else is required. Durga herself appears when the gods are under siege and beg the divine feminine for help. She arrives fully armed, riding a lion, her many hands holding weapons gifted by each god who could not wield them effectively himself. She is not gentle. She does not negotiate. She ends the threat. What both goddesses teach is this: there are moments when rage is the only appropriate response. When a boundary has been violated. When harm is ongoing. When you have been diminished, silenced, or erased. In those moments, your anger is not a problem to be managed. It is intelligence. It is life force. It is the part of you that knows you deserve better and will not be talked out of it. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the idea that anger is diagnostic. It tells you where your limits are. It tells you what you value. It tells you when something is wrong. Kali and Durga make that teaching visceral. They show you what it looks like to trust your rage enough to act on it. ## What Happens When We Silence the Fire Somatic psychology has spent decades documenting what happens when anger is chronically suppressed. Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, describes how incomplete defensive responses, like the impulse to push away or strike out that gets aborted mid-movement, remain stored in the nervous system as chronic tension, bracing, and hypervigilance. Your body remembers what it was not allowed to do. You can feel this in your shoulders, which have been raised in a defensive hunch for so long you don't notice anymore. In your jaw, clenched tight enough to crack teeth. In your gut, where the fire you swallowed burns as acid reflux or irritable bowel syndrome. The anger doesn't go away when you refuse it. It just goes underground and becomes pathology. PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load shows that people who habitually suppress anger show elevated blood pressure, increased cortisol, and higher incidence of heart disease. Your heart, quite literally, cannot sustain the effort of holding all that fire inside. Karla McLaren, who has written extensively on the intelligence of emotions, reframes anger as the boundary-setting emotion. It arises to tell you that something is not okay, that a line has been crossed, that restoration is needed. When you ignore that signal, you lose access to your own limits. You become porous. You say yes when you mean no. You tolerate the intolerable. And your body pays the price. In my own practice, I have watched people discover that their chronic neck pain is not a mechanical problem but a held "no" that was never spoken. That their migraines are the pressure of years of unvoiced objection. That their autoimmune flares correlate directly with periods of self-abandonment. The body keeps the score, and anger unexpressed becomes inflammation, pain, disease. ## Durga's Weapons: The Somatic Tools of Healthy Rage Durga holds a different weapon in each of her ten hands, and each one represents a different capacity. The sword of discernment. The trident of focused intention. The bow of aim. The lotus of compassion even in battle. She teaches that anger is not one thing. It is a spectrum of responses, and you get to choose which tool fits the moment. This is where somatic practice becomes essential. You cannot think your way into healthy anger. You have to feel it, move it, let it live in your body without either suppressing it or letting it run wild. One practice you can try right now: Stand with your feet hip-width apart. Let your knees soften. Take a breath and imagine something that genuinely angers you, something where a boundary was crossed. Notice where you feel that in your body. Is it heat in your chest? Tightness in your fists? A clenching in your belly? Now, very slowly, let your hands rise in front of you as if you are pressing against an invisible wall. Push. Feel the strength in your arms, your core, your legs. Say out loud, "No. This is not okay." Push harder. Let your voice get louder. Stay with it for thirty seconds. Then release. Shake out your hands. Notice what shifted. This is not about screaming at anyone. It is about completing the movement your nervous system wanted to make and was not allowed to. It is about letting your body know that it has the right, and the capacity, to defend itself. Some people find it helpful to support the nervous system during this kind of work with gentle supplements. [Natural Vitality Calm Magnesium Powder, Raspberry Lemon, 16oz](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00BPUY3W0?tag=spankyspinola-20) can ease the muscle tension that comes with chronic bracing, while [Solgar GABA 500 mg (100 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0001OP014?tag=spankyspinola-20) supports the calming neurotransmitter that helps you come back down after activation. These are not fixes, but they can make the practice more sustainable. ## Kali's Tongue: The Sound of Unfiltered Truth Kali is often shown with her tongue out, and there are many interpretations of this. One is that she is tasting the blood of demons. Another is that she has just realized she is dancing on her beloved Shiva and is shocked into stillness. But another, older reading is this: her tongue is out because she is speaking the unspeakable. She is saying what cannot be said in polite company. She is the voice that will not be modulated, softened, or made palatable. Soraya Chemaly, in her work on women and anger, points out that girls are socialized from infancy to suppress their anger in ways boys are not. They are taught that anger makes them ugly, shrill, hysterical, unfeminine. They learn to perform pleasantness even when they are seething. And this has profound consequences not just for individual health but for systemic power. When half the population is trained not to express anger, they are also trained not to resist, not to demand, not to fight back. Kali refuses that training. She is the part of you that will not be quiet. The part that knows the emperor has no clothes and will say so. The part that would rather be cast out than complicit. You have that part too. It lives in the words you have bitten back a thousand times. In the emails you drafted and never sent. In the conversations you replay in your head where you finally say what you really think. Kali invites you to stop rehearsing and start speaking. This does not mean you scream at everyone who annoys you. It means you stop lying about what you feel. It means you practice saying, "I'm angry about this," without softening it into, "I'm a little frustrated." It means you let your voice carry the full weight of your conviction. If you have been interested in exploring more tools for working with anger in embodied, sustainable ways, [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) offers guided practices for exactly this kind of reclamation. ## The Body Remembers What the Mind Forgets Your body holds the history of every time you wanted to push back and didn't. Every time you smiled through gritted teeth. Every time you made yourself small to keep someone else comfortable. That history lives in your fascia, your muscle memory, your nervous system's default settings. Over the years I've seen people come into a somatic anger practice and discover sensations they did not know they were carrying. A burning in the solar plexus that has been there since childhood. A tightness across the shoulder blades like invisible hands holding them back. A chronic nausea that appears whenever they are about to set a boundary. These are not random symptoms. They are the body's way of saying: I have something to tell you. I have been trying to tell you for years. Will you listen now? Listening looks like this: you slow down enough to feel. You put your hand on your belly and notice the clench. You breathe into your chest and feel the heat. You let your fists close and open, close and open, until the tremor that wants to move through you is allowed to move. You might shake. You might cry. You might feel a sudden rush of energy that makes you want to run or hit a pillow or roar. All of that is welcome. All of that is the anger coming back online. Some people find that adaptogenic support helps them stay present during this work without becoming overwhelmed. [Gaia Herbs Stress Response](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005DROYSO?tag=spankyspinola-20) is designed to support resilience during periods of high emotional activation, and can be a useful ally when you are working with intense somatic release. ## Rage as Devotion Here is the thing about Kali and Durga that gets missed in the West: they are not destructive for destruction's sake. Kali destroys ego, illusion, the false self that keeps you small. Durga destroys the forces that threaten the innocent. Both are acts of love. Both are in service to life. Your anger, when it is clear and embodied, is the same. It is not petty. It is not cruel. It is the part of you that loves yourself, and others, enough to refuse harm. It is the part of you that will protect the vulnerable, including the vulnerable parts of yourself. It is fierce, yes. But it is also holy. This is the spiritual permission the goddesses offer. You do not have to transcend your anger to be whole. You do not have to rise above it or let it go before you have fully felt it. You can let it burn through you, let it clarify what matters, let it carve out the space you need to live with integrity. In our experience, the people who do this work, who stop apologizing for their anger and start trusting it, become more present, not less. More compassionate, not less. Because when you are not spending all your energy suppressing your fire, you have energy for everything else. For joy. For connection. For the kind of fierce tenderness that can hold both rage and love at the same time. If you are curious about how we approach this work more broadly, you can learn more about our framework on [the about page](/about), or explore practices tailored to your specific nervous system patterns through [the assessments hub](/assessments). ## A Practice: Kali's Breath Sit comfortably. Close your eyes. Bring to mind a situation where you feel angry, where a boundary was crossed or you were diminished. Let yourself feel it. Do not edit. Do not make it smaller. Now, open your mouth wide. Stick out your tongue as far as it will go, the way Kali does. Breathe in through your nose, and then exhale forcefully through your open mouth with your tongue out, making a "haaaa" sound. Let it be loud. Let it be fierce. Do this ten times. Notice what you feel. Heat? Tingling? A sense of release? This is a traditional practice in some tantric lineages, designed to activate and move stuck energy. It is simple, but it works. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Is it really okay to feel this angry, or am I just being dramatic? Your anger is information. It is telling you that something matters to you, that a line has been crossed, that restoration is needed. The question is not whether the anger is justified, but whether you are willing to listen to what it is trying to tell you. Dismissing your own anger as drama is a way of dismissing yourself. The goddesses would not do that, and neither should you. ### What if my anger scares me or feels too big to handle? Anger that feels overwhelming is often anger that has been suppressed for a long time and is finally breaking through. It is not too big. You are just not used to feeling it. The practice is to let it move in small, contained ways - through breath, through movement, through sound - so that it does not have to explode. Working with a somatic therapist or practitioner trained in trauma-informed anger work can be invaluable here. ### How do I express anger without hurting people I care about? Healthy anger is not about attacking. It is about clarity. You can say, "I am angry about this, and here is what I need," without blaming or shaming. You can set a boundary without making the other person wrong. You can let your anger inform your action without letting it control your action. The goddesses are fierce, but they are also precise. They destroy what needs to be destroyed, not everything in sight. ## The Dance Continues Kali dances on the body of Shiva, and in some stories, she dances until the world ends. In others, Shiva's stillness beneath her feet reminds her to stop, to come back, to balance destruction with creation. The teaching is not that you should be angry all the time. It is that you should be willing to be angry when anger is what is needed, and then let it go when it has done its work. Your rage is not your enemy. It is your guardian. It is the part of you that will not let you be erased. And when you learn to move with it instead of against it, when you give it the space and the voice and the embodied expression it needs, something shifts. You become less reactive, not more. You become more discerning. You become capable of the kind of fierce love that can hold boundaries and compassion at the same time. The goddesses are still here. They are in the heat rising in your chest when someone crosses a line. They are in the words you have been afraid to say. They are in the part of you that knows, without question, that you deserve better. Listen to them. Let them teach you. Let your anger become what it was always meant to be: not a problem to solve, but a power to reclaim. For more explorations of the body's wisdom and the practices that honor it, visit [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), where we continue this conversation in different forms. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on kali, durga, and the spiritual permission of rage, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
